docs.title: Coding Cookbook
docs.text: This is where I'll host snippets of code for doing certain things because I don't want to keep googling or asking AI everytime I forget.

You have to pay to get manga from these websites, tofugu has a guide on how to do that. Sometimes these websites will also offer up some books/chapters for free (usually for a limited time) so it is also worth checking out even if you are not willing to spend some money.

docs.text:
Dictionary
  • goo - discontinued
  • jpdb
  • weblio
  • kotobank
Character Extraction/Recogntion
  • mokuro - manga OCR
  • YomiNinja - screen OCR
  • textractor - extract text from VNs
  • yomitan - pop-up dictionary for browser
Video Players

asbplayer : A tool to add subtitles to online/streaming videos. The website jimaku is a good source of kanji subtitles for anime, etc..

Example sentence search

In google, twitter, etc.. Use quotation marks to surround a word/phrase to find examples of sentences containing them. Alternatively, the websites below are also useful

  • immersionkit - examples from anime
  • youglish - examples from YouTube
  • sentencesearch
  • massif.la
  • kanshudo
  • eijirou

docs.title: Blog
docs.text:
If I come across something cool, I'll try to write about it over here. Each post is sorted into categories (check the about page).

Warning

For STEM stuff especially, I try my best with research, but I am no professional so there may be some inaccuracies here and there with what I say.

docs.title: Maths
docs.text: Exactly what it says. Mostly, this will be a place where I can document and keep track of some ideas/concepts.

docs.title: Data Science / Statistics
docs.text: Exactly what it says. Mostly, this will be a place where I can document and keep track of some ideas/concepts.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text: A place where I'll document some projects (mostly coding) that I have been working on.

docs.title: Japanese
docs.text: Mostly stuff about the language since I am learning it at the moment, but can be about some bits of culture/history that I find cool too.

docs.title: Things I Like Corner
docs.text: A place where I'll share some of the stuff that I enjoy consuming. This includes, but is not limited to : cool YT videos, music, animation, anime, BL, character art, etc..

docs.title: Miscellaneous
docs.text: Anything that does not fit into the above categories

docs.title: いーあるふぁんくらぶ (Cantonese version)
docs.text:
いーあるふぁんくらぶ is a vocaloid song by mikitoP. The song itself is about learning Chinese, and so it has gained quite the popularity in China, to the point that Chinese versions of the song have been made.

Even cooler than that is the Cantonese version of the song by Hane (embedded above). The song is mainly in Cantonese, with some Chinese and Japanese, and a sentence of English, making it consist of 4 languages in total (very cool). I was rather upset that there weren't any lyrics available for this version of the song so I decided to transcribe it myself. I'm not confident enough to translate them to English, but you can use CC-Canto to check the meanings out and explore for yourself.

docs.title: 斯々然々
docs.text:
I came across an interesting-sounding word while watching episode 8 of Youkai Gakkou no Sensei Hajimemashita! :

The anime was even kind enough to provide an explanation, and I thought that was funny.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text:
斯々然々 (かくかくしかじか) is often used in media as a practical phrase that an author can use in place of some long explanation that the reader/viewer might already be aware of. For example, imagine the following situation :

  • Scene 1 : Something happens to character A
  • Scene 2 : Character B shows up and asks what happened
  • Scene 3 : Character A explains the situation

Now, the author may make "Character A" go through the whole song and dance of explaining the situation (which the readers most likely don't want), or they may simply make "Character A" say something like「まぁ。。かくかくしかじかでこうなちゃった」

It can also be used (though I am not sure how commonly) in real-life conversations. For example, you can use it whenever you want to explain yourself without revealing any details. Here are some examples1 :

  • 斯々然々ありまして…離婚する事になりました。
  • 斯々然々の事情があって、退職する事になった。
  • 斯々然々あって、遅れちゃったの。ごめんね。

The fact that the anime felt the need to explain what this phrase meant seems to suggest that it is not often used in everyday life.

docs.title: Pop Culture
docs.text: かくかくしかじか has been used as a title for a certain manga. It is also the name of a deer mascot character used by Daihatsu for advertisments and such. This is probably a pun, $\stackrel{\tiny しか}{\footnotesize 鹿}$ being the kanji for deer.

docs.title: History
docs.text: In the anime, it was also mentioned that かくかくしかじか was used in the 万葉集 (Man'yōshū), an 8th century anthology of Japanese poetry. Both かくかく and しかじか are archaic ways of saying こうだ/このように2 and so it seems that people of the time decided to combine them to create the かくかくしかじか that we know of today. It is one of the few phrases from Old Japanese to have persisted all the way to the modern age.

docs.title: Reference :
docs.text:
1. https://ja.hinative.com/questions/19937586 ↩

  2. https://www.tbs.co.jp/kodomotel/etc/0088.html ↩

  3. https://d.hatena.ne.jp/keyword/かくかくしかじか ↩

  4. https://dic.pixiv.net/a/かくかくしかじか ↩
